docs.title: Why Crablet?
docs.text:
Crablet is built from scratch in Rust, delivering unmatched performance and reliability for AI agent infrastructure:

Aspect Benefit Performance Native Rust performance — 3-5× faster than Node.js alternatives Safety Compile-time guarantees, no null pointers, no data races Concurrency Tokio async runtime supporting 100+ concurrent agents Reliability Type-safe error handling with anyhow + thiserror Extensibility Plugin architecture with Skills, Tools, MCP, and Lua scripting Deployment Docker images < 20 MB, startup < 500 ms

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
CLI ChatWeb UIDockerRust API 
# Interactive conversation
crablet chat

# Single command execution
crablet run "Summarize the latest news about Rust"

# Start unified Web UI + API gateway
crablet serve-web --port 18790

# Or explicit gateway mode
crablet gateway --port 18790

docker run -d \
  --name crablet \
  -p 18790:18790 \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-xxx \
  crablet:latest

use crablet::prelude::*;

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<()> {
    let mut agent = Crablet::new(config?).await?;

    let response = agent
        .chat("Explain Rust ownership")
        .with_model("gpt-4o")
        .with_tools(true)
        .send()
        .await?;

    println!("{}", response.content);
    Ok(())
}

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name crablet \
  -p 18790:18790 \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-xxx \
  -v ./data:/data \
  -v ./skills:/skills \
  crablet:latest

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
version: '3.8'

services:
  crablet:
    image: crablet:latest
    ports:
      - "18790:18790"      # Web UI
      - "18789:18789"      # Gateway
    environment:
      - OPENAI_API_KEY=${OPENAI_API_KEY}
      - DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///data/crablet.db
      - RUST_LOG=info
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
      - ./skills:/skills
      - ./config:/root/.config/crablet
    depends_on:
      - neo4j
    restart: unless-stopped

  neo4j:
    image: neo4j:5
    ports:
      - "7474:7474"
      - "7687:7687"
    environment:
      - NEO4J_AUTH=neo4j/password
    volumes:
      - neo4j_data:/data

volumes:
  neo4j_data:

docs.title: Building Custom Images
docs.text:
FROM rust:1.82-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN cargo build --release --no-default-features --features web,knowledge,telemetry

FROM alpine:3.19
COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/crablet /usr/local/bin/
COPY config/ /etc/crablet/
EXPOSE 18790
ENTRYPOINT ["crablet", "serve-web", "--port", "18790"]

# alerts.yaml
groups:
  - name: crablet
    rules:
      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: rate(crablet_tool_errors[5m]) > 0.1
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning

      - alert: LLMCostSpike
        expr: increase(crablet_llm_tokens[1h]) > 1000000

        labels:

docs.title: Production Configuration
docs.text:
# config.toml (production)
database_url = "postgresql://user:pass@localhost/crablet"
log_level = "warn"

[safety]
level = "Strict"
allowed_commands = ["ls", "cat", "grep", "find", "git", "cargo"]
blocked_commands = ["rm -rf", "mkfs", "dd", "format"]

[telemetry]
enabled = true
endpoint = "http://tempo:4317"

[limits]
max_concurrent_requests = 100
request_timeout = 30

[cache]
enabled = true
ttl_seconds = 3600
max_entries = 10000

docs.title: Vertical Scaling
docs.text:
• Increase max_concurrent_requests based on CPU cores
  • Tune tokio worker threads: TOKIO_WORKER_THREADS=8
  • Allocate sufficient memory for vector indices

docs.title: Horizontal Scaling
docs.text:
• Use PostgreSQL for shared state
  • Deploy behind a load balancer (nginx/HAProxy)
  • Sticky sessions for WebSocket connections
  • Shared storage for skills and knowledge bases

docs.title: Adding Channels
docs.text: Connect Crablet to new messaging platforms by implementing the Channel trait.

docs.title: Channel Trait
docs.text:
#[async_trait]
pub trait Channel: Send + Sync {
    /// Send a message through this channel
    async fn send_message(&self, msg: &str) -> Result<()>;

    /// Receive incoming messages (non-blocking)
    async fn receive_message(&self) -> Result<Option<IncomingMessage>>;

    /// Start the channel event loop
    async fn start(self: Box<Self>) -> Result<()>;

    /// Human-readable channel name
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
}

docs.title: Example: Slack Channel
docs.text:
// src/channels/slack/mod.rs
use crate::channels::Channel;
use async_trait::async_trait;
use anyhow::Result;

pub struct SlackChannel {
    client: slack::Client,
    config: SlackConfig,
}

#[async_trait]
impl Channel for SlackChannel {
    fn name(&self) -> &str { "slack" }

    async fn send_message(&self, msg: &str) -> Result<()> {
        self.client.post_message(&self.config.channel_id, msg).await?;
        Ok(())
    }

    async fn receive_message(&self) -> Result<Option<IncomingMessage>> {
        // Poll or listen for incoming Slack messages
        let msg = self.client.poll_message().await?;
        Ok(msg.map(|m| IncomingMessage {
            content: m.text,
            sender: m.user,
            channel: self.name().to_string(),
        }))
    }

    async fn start(self: Box<Self>) -> Result<()> {
        // Start Slack WebSocket connection
        self.client.connect().await?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

docs.title: Registering Your Channel
docs.text:
Add to src/channels/mod.rs:

mod slack;

pub fn register_channels(registry: &mut ChannelRegistry, config: &Config) {
    if config.channels.slack.enabled {
        registry.register(slack::SlackChannel::new(config));
    }
}

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Test your channel with mock implementations:

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_slack_send() {
        let channel = SlackChannel::new(test_config());
        let result = channel.send_message("Test message").await;
        assert!(result.is_ok());
    }
}

docs.title: Adding Tools
docs.text: Extend Crablet's capabilities by implementing new built-in tools.

docs.title: Plugin Trait
docs.text:
All tools implement the Plugin trait:

use crate::plugins::Plugin;
use async_trait::async_trait;
use anyhow::Result;

#[async_trait]
pub trait Plugin: Send + Sync {
    /// Unique tool identifier
    fn name(&self) -> &str;

    /// Human-readable description (shown to LLM)
    fn description(&self) -> &str;

    /// JSON Schema for input parameters
    fn parameters_schema(&self) -> serde_json::Value;

    /// Execute the tool
    async fn execute(&self, input: &str) -> Result<String>;

    /// Whether this tool requires safety approval
    fn requires_approval(&self) -> bool {
        false
    }
}

docs.title: Example: DNS Lookup Tool
docs.text:
// src/tools/dns_lookup.rs
use crate::plugins::Plugin;
use async_trait::async_trait;
use anyhow::Result;
use serde_json::json;

pub struct DnsLookup;

#[async_trait]
impl Plugin for DnsLookup {
    fn name(&self) -> &str { "dns_lookup" }

    fn description(&self) -> &str {
        "Look up DNS records for a domain name"
    }

    fn parameters_schema(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
        json!({
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "domain": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "Domain name to look up"
                },
                "record_type": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "enum": ["A", "AAAA", "MX", "TXT", "CNAME"],
                    "default": "A"
                }
            },
            "required": ["domain"]
        })
    }

    async fn execute(&self, input: &str) -> Result<String> {
        let args: DnsArgs = serde_json::from_str(input)?;
        // Perform DNS lookup...
        Ok(result)
    }

    fn requires_approval(&self) -> bool {
        false  // DNS lookups are safe
    }
}

docs.title: Registering Your Tool
docs.text:
Add your tool to the registry in src/tools/mod.rs:

mod dns_lookup;

pub fn register_all(registry: &mut ToolRegistry) {
    registry.register(dns_lookup::DnsLookup);
    // ... other tools
}

docs.text:
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_dns_lookup() {

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
• Always add #[cfg(test)] unit tests

  • Pass cargo clippy without warnings
  • Handle errors gracefully with anyhow
  • Respect the Safety Oracle for dangerous operations

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Crablet follows a modular, layered architecture built entirely in Rust on top of the Tokio async runtime.

docs.title: System Panorama
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph Application Layer
        CH[Channels<br/>CLI/Web/Telegram/Discord]
        GW[Gateway<br/>REST/WebSocket/JSON-RPC]
    end

    subgraph Cognitive Layer
        CR[Cognitive Router]
        S1[System 1<br/>Fast Intuition]
        S2[System 2<br/>Slow Reasoning]
        S3[System 3<br/>Meta-Cognition]
    end

    subgraph Memory Layer
        WM[Working Memory]
        EM[Episodic Memory]
        SM[Semantic Memory]
        PM[Procedural Memory]
        MF[Memory Fusion]
    end

    subgraph Infrastructure Layer
        LL[LLM Adapters<br/>OpenAI/DashScope/Ollama]
        TL[Tool Registry<br/>Bash/File/Web/Browser]
        DB[Storage<br/>SQLite/Neo4j/Qdrant]
        SF[Safety Oracle]
        OB[Observability<br/>OpenTelemetry]
    end

    CH --> CR
    GW --> CR
    CR --> S1
    CR --> S2
    CR --> S3
    S2 --> TL
    TL --> SF
    S3 --> MF
    MF --> WM
    MF --> EM
    MF --> SM
    MF --> PM
    S1 --> LL
    S2 --> LL
    TL --> DB

docs.title: Why Rust?
docs.text:
• Zero-cost abstractions — High-level ergonomics without runtime overhead
  • Fearless concurrency — Tokio async runtime with compile-time safety
  • Small binaries — Docker images < 20 MB
  • Fast startup — < 500ms cold start

docs.title: Why Tokio?
docs.text:
• Industry-standard async runtime for Rust
  • Supports 100+ concurrent agents efficiently
  • Mature ecosystem with tracing, tower, hyper

docs.title: Why anyhow + thiserror?
docs.text:
• anyhow for application-level error propagation
  • thiserror for library-level typed errors
  • Consistent pattern across the entire codebase

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant CH as Channel
    participant CR as Cognitive Router
    participant S1 as System 1/2
    participant TL as Tools
    participant SF as Safety Oracle
    participant MF as Memory Fusion
    participant LL as LLM

    U->>CH: Message
    CH->>CR: Route
    CR->>MF: Retrieve context
    MF-->>CR: Relevant memories
    CR->>LL: Generate response
    LL-->>CR: Raw response
    CR->>TL: Execute tools (if needed)
    TL->>SF: Validate
    SF-->>TL: Approved
    TL-->>CR: Tool results
    CR->>LL: Continue with results
    LL-->>CH: Final response
    CH->>U: Reply

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Crablet! This guide will help you get started.

docs.title: Development Environment
docs.text:
Prerequisites:

  • Rust 1.80+ (via rustup)
  • Docker (optional, for sandbox and Neo4j)
  • sccache (recommended, speeds up compilation)
# Clone and build
git clone https://github.com/isLinXu/crablet.git
cd crablet

# Minimal build (recommended for daily dev)
cargo build --no-default-features --features web

# Full build
cargo build --release

# Run tests
cargo test --release

# Lint
cargo clippy -- -D warnings

# Format check
cargo fmt --check

docs.title: Most Welcome Contributions
docs.text: Area Path Difficulty New tool plugins src/tools/ New channel adapters src/channels/ Cognitive middleware src/cognitive/middleware.rs Skill packages skills/ LLM adapters src/cognitive/llm/ Documentation docs/

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text:
Use GitHub Issues with the Bug Report template. Include:

  • Crablet version (crablet --version)
  • OS and Rust version
  • Minimal reproduction steps
  • Expected vs actual behavior

docs.title: Submitting Pull Requests
docs.text:
1. Fork and create a branch: git checkout -b feature/my-feature
  2. Write code and tests
  3. Ensure all checks pass: cargo test && cargo clippy && cargo fmt --check
  4. Submit PR with a clear description

docs.title: Commit Convention
docs.text:
feat: add weather tool with OpenMeteo API
fix: resolve memory leak in working memory consolidation

docs.title: Code Standards
docs.text:
• Follow Rust standard style (cargo fmt)
  • Add doc comments to all public API
  • New features must include unit or integration tests
  • Pass cargo clippy without warnings

docs.title: Creating Skills
docs.text: Skills are the primary extension mechanism for Crablet. This guide covers creating both executable and instructional skills.

docs.title: Skill Types Comparison
docs.text: Aspect Executable (skill.yaml) Instructional (SKILL.md) Runtime Python/Node/Shell None (pure prompt) Complexity Medium-High Low Flexibility Full programming power Behavioral instructions only Distribution Git repo or zip Git repo or zip Testing Automated Manual review

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
my-skill/
├── skill.yaml          # Manifest
├── main.py             # Entry point
├── requirements.txt    # Dependencies (optional)
└── README.md           # Documentation

docs.title: Manifest (skill.yaml)
docs.text:
name: my-skill
description: What this skill does
version: 1.0.0
author: Your Name

parameters:
  type: object
  properties:
    input:
      type: string
      description: Input description
  required: [input]

entrypoint: python3 main.py
timeout: 30

# Optional environment variables
env:
  API_KEY: ${MY_API_KEY}

# Optional dependencies
dependencies:
  - name: another-skill
    version: ">=1.0.0"

docs.title: Entry Point
docs.text:
Your script receives arguments as JSON via sys.argv[1] and prints results to stdout:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
import json

def main():
    args = json.loads(sys.argv[1])
    result = process(args["input"])
    print(json.dumps({"result": result}))

def process(input_text):
    # Your logic here
    return input_text.upper()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: Creating an Instructional Skill
docs.text:
Simply write a SKILL.md with frontmatter:

---
name: rust-expert
description: Expert Rust programming assistant
version: 1.0.0
author: Your Name
tags: [coding, rust, systems-programming]
---

You are an expert Rust programmer. Follow these principles:

1. **Ownership First** — Always consider ownership and borrowing
2. **Error Handling** — Use `Result<T, E>` with `thiserror` for libraries
3. **Async** — Prefer `tokio` for async operations
4. **Testing** — Write tests for all public functions
5. **Documentation** — Add rustdoc comments to all public items

When reviewing code:
- Check for unnecessary `clone()` calls
- Verify `unsafe` blocks are justified
- Ensure proper error propagation

docs.title: Publishing
docs.text:
# Publish to a Git repository
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial skill release"
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/my-skill.git
git push -u origin main

Users can then install:

crablet skill install https://github.com/you/my-skill.git

docs.title: :filter: Middleware
docs.text: Cognitive middleware allows you to intercept and modify the agent's thinking process at various stages.

docs.title: CognitiveMiddleware Trait
docs.text:
#[async_trait]
pub trait CognitiveMiddleware: Send + Sync {
    /// Name of this middleware
    fn name(&self) -> &str;

    /// Pre-process a request before the cognitive engine
    async fn pre_process(&self, request: &mut CognitiveRequest) -> Result<MiddlewareAction>;

    /// Post-process a response after the cognitive engine
    async fn post_process(&self, response: &mut CognitiveResponse) -> Result<()>;
}

pub enum MiddlewareAction {
    Continue,           // Pass through to next middleware
    Skip,               // Skip remaining middleware
    Abort(String),      // Abort with error message
    Redirect(CognitiveLayer),  // Redirect to different cognitive layer
}

docs.title: Example: Rate Limiting Middleware
docs.text:
pub struct RateLimitMiddleware {
    limiter: RateLimiter,
}

#[async_trait]
impl CognitiveMiddleware for RateLimitMiddleware {
    fn name(&self) -> &str { "rate_limit" }

    async fn pre_process(&self, request: &mut CognitiveRequest) -> Result<MiddlewareAction> {
        if !self.limiter.allow() {
            return Ok(MiddlewareAction::Abort(
                "Rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.".to_string()
            ));
        }
        Ok(MiddlewareAction::Continue)
    }

    async fn post_process(&self, _response: &mut CognitiveResponse) -> Result<()> {
        Ok(())
    }
}

docs.title: Registering Middleware
docs.text:
// In your initialization code
engine.add_middleware(RateLimitMiddleware::new(config));
engine.add_middleware(LoggingMiddleware::new());
engine.add_middleware(CachingMiddleware::new(cache));

docs.title: Execution Order
docs.text:
Middleware executes in registration order for pre-processing, and reverse order for post-processing:

Pre:  MW1 → MW2 → MW3 → [Engine]
Post: [Engine] → MW3 → MW2 → MW1

docs.title: What is Crablet?
docs.text: Crablet is a production-ready AI Agent Operating System built from scratch in Rust. It provides a complete framework for building, deploying, and managing AI agents with a three-layer cognitive architecture, four-layer memory system, and multi-channel support.

docs.title: Why is it called Crablet?
docs.text: Because good AI infrastructure should be as reliable as a crab's shell, and as fast as it scuttles sideways. 🦀

docs.title: How does Crablet compare to other agent frameworks?
docs.text: Feature Crablet OpenClaw Agent Zero Language Rust TypeScript Python Performance ★★★★★ ★★★ ★★ Memory System 4-layer 2-layer Basic Cognitive Architecture 3-layer None None Concurrency 100+ agents ~10 ~5 Binary Size < 20 MB ~100 MB ~50 MB

docs.title: Which LLM providers are supported?
docs.text:
• OpenAI (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, etc.)
  • DashScope (Qwen series)
  • Ollama (Local models)
  • Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint

docs.title: Can I use Crablet without Docker?
docs.text: Yes! Crablet runs natively on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Docker is only needed for the sandbox feature.

docs.title: How do I reduce memory usage?
docs.text:
# Build with minimal features
cargo build --release --no-default-features --features web

# Reduce working memory capacity in config
[memory.working]
capacity = 30  # Default is 50-100

docs.title: Is Crablet production-ready?
docs.text: Crablet has comprehensive test coverage (347+ test functions, 40+ integration test files) and CI/CD pipelines. It's suitable for production deployment with proper configuration.

docs.title: Build fails with "linker not found"
docs.text:
Install a C compiler and linker:

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install build-essential

# macOS
xcode-select --install

docs.title: WebSocket connection refused
docs.text:
Ensure the gateway is running:

crablet gateway --port 18789
# Or
crablet serve-web --port 18790

docs.title: LLM returns errors
docs.text:
Check your API key configuration:

echo $OPENAI_API_KEY
# Should output: sk-xxx...

docs.title: High memory usage
docs.text:
Reduce vector index sizes and working memory capacity:

[memory.working]
capacity = 30

[knowledge]
chunk_size = 256  # Smaller chunks = less memory
top_k = 5         # Fewer retrieved results

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Crablet is highly configurable through TOML config files, environment variables, and compile-time feature flags.

docs.title: Config File
docs.text:
Default location: ~/.config/crablet/config.toml

# Database connection
database_url = "sqlite:crablet.db?mode=rwc"

# LLM provider
model_name = "gpt-4o-mini"
max_tokens = 4096
temperature = 0.7

# Logging
log_level = "info"  # trace | debug | info | warn | error

# Safety policies
[safety]
level = "Strict"  # Strict | Permissive | Disabled
allowed_commands = ["ls", "cat", "echo", "git", "cargo"]
blocked_commands = ["rm -rf", "mkfs", "dd"]

# MCP servers
[mcp_servers]
math_server = { command = "python3", args = ["mcp_server.py"] }

# Skills directory
skills_dir = "skills"

# Rate limits
[limits]
max_concurrent_requests = 100
request_timeout = 30

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Environment variables override config file settings:

Variable Description OPENAI_API_KEY OpenAI API key DASHSCOPE_API_KEY Alibaba Cloud DashScope API key OPENAI_API_BASE OpenAI-compatible API endpoint URL OLLAMA_MODEL Local Ollama model name SERPER_API_KEY Serper search API key DATABASE_URL Database connection string RUST_LOG Log level override GRAPH_RAG_ENTITY_MODE Entity extraction mode: rule | phrase | hybrid (default: hybrid)

docs.title: Feature Flags
docs.text:
Compile-time feature flags allow you to trim Crablet to only the capabilities you need:

Feature Includes Default web Web UI, API gateway knowledge RAG, vector storage, Neo4j, Qdrant audio Whisper speech recognition, TTS scripting Lua 5.4 scripting engine telemetry OpenTelemetry tracing sandbox Docker sandbox execution telegram Telegram Bot channel discord Discord Bot channel browser Headless browser automation Minimal BuildFull BuildProduction Server 
# Only Web UI + scripting
cargo build --release --no-default-features --features web,scripting

# Everything enabled (default)
cargo build --release

# Server-focused: no audio/browser, with telemetry
cargo build --release --no-default-features \
  --features web,knowledge,telemetry,sandbox

docs.title: Safety Levels
docs.text:
Crablet provides three safety modes for tool execution:

StrictPermissiveDisabled
  • All shell commands require approval
  • File access restricted to allowed paths
  • No network access from sandbox
[safety]
level = "Strict"
allowed_commands = ["ls", "cat", "head", "tail", "grep", "find"]
  • Known-safe commands auto-approved
  • File access limited to home directory tree
  • Network allowed with rate limiting
[safety]
level = "Permissive"
blocked_commands = ["rm -rf /", "mkfs", "dd"]
  • All commands auto-approved
  • Full filesystem access
  • Unrestricted network
[safety]
level = "Disabled"

Use with caution

Disabled mode should only be used in trusted, isolated environments.

docs.title: Option 1: Build from Source
docs.text:
Full BuildMinimal BuildCustom Features 
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/isLinXu/crablet.git
cd crablet

# Full build with all features (~15-20 min)
cargo build --release

# Initialize configuration
./target/release/crablet init

git clone https://github.com/isLinXu/crablet.git
cd crablet

# Minimal build — CLI + Web only (~5 min)
cargo build --release --no-default-features --features web

./target/release/crablet init

# Pick only the features you need
cargo build --release --no-default-features \
  --features web,knowledge,scripting

# Available features:
#   web         — Web UI + API gateway
#   knowledge   — RAG, vector storage, Neo4j, Qdrant
#   audio       — Whisper speech recognition + TTS
#   scripting   — Lua 5.4 scripting engine
#   telemetry   — OpenTelemetry tracing
#   sandbox     — Docker sandbox execution
#   telegram    — Telegram Bot channel
#   discord     — Discord Bot channel
#   browser     — Headless browser automation
Speed up compilation with sccache

Install sccache to cache compiled artifacts:

cargo install sccache
export RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache

Subsequent builds can be 5-10× faster.

docs.title: Option 2: Docker
docs.text:
Docker RunDocker Compose 
docker run -d \
  --name crablet \
  -p 18790:18790 \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-xxx \
  -v ./data:/data \
  -v ./skills:/skills \
  crablet:latest

# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'

services:
  crablet:
    image: crablet:latest
    ports:
      - "18790:18790"
    environment:
      - OPENAI_API_KEY=${OPENAI_API_KEY}
      - DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///data/crablet.db
    volumes:
      - ./data:/data
      - ./skills:/skills

  neo4j:  # Optional — for graph RAG
    image: neo4j:5
    ports:
      - "7474:7474"
      - "7687:7687"
    environment:
      - NEO4J_AUTH=neo4j/password

docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Step 1: Set Your API Key
docs.text:
Crablet needs an LLM provider to function. Set at least one:

OpenAIDashScope (阿里云)Ollama (Local)Custom Endpoint

ollama pull llama3

export OLLAMA_MODEL=llama3

export OPENAI_API_KEY=your-key

Interactive ChatSingle CommandWeb UI

docs.title: :terminal: CLI Usage
docs.text: Crablet provides a rich CLI for interactive conversations, single-shot execution, and system management.

docs.title: Interactive Chat
docs.text:
crablet chat

Start an interactive REPL-style conversation. Supports:

  • Multi-turn dialogue with context retention
  • Inline tool execution (shell, file, web search)
  • Cognitive layer switching (System ½/3)
  • Session persistence

docs.title: Single-Shot Execution
docs.text:
crablet run "Summarize this paper about transformers"

Execute a single prompt and return the result. Useful for scripting and pipelines:

# Pipe input
echo "Translate to French:" | crablet run "$(cat -)"

# Chain with other tools
crablet run "List TODOs in this project" | grep -i urgent

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts (Chat Mode)
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+C Cancel current generation Ctrl+D Exit chat ↑ / ↓ Navigate command history Tab Auto-complete commands /help Show in-chat help /clear Clear conversation history /model <name> Switch LLM model /tools Toggle tool usage

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Crablet takes security seriously with a multi-layered approach to protect your system and data.

docs.title: Safety Oracle
docs.text:
The Safety Oracle validates all tool executions before they run:

graph LR
    A[Agent Request] --> B{Safety Oracle}
    B -->|Approved| C[Execute Tool]
    B -->|Denied| D[Return Error]
    B -->|Needs Approval| E[Prompt User]
    E -->|Approved| C
    E -->|Denied| D

docs.title: Validation Layers
docs.text:
1. Command Validation — Checks against allowed/blocked command lists
  2. Path Sandboxing — Restricts file access to permitted directories
  3. Network Policies — Controls outbound connections
  4. Resource Limits — Enforces CPU, memory, and time constraints

docs.title: DM Pairing (Bot Mode)
docs.text:
When Crablet runs as a messaging bot, unknown users must be approved:

1. Unknown user sends message → Crablet generates 6-digit pairing code
2. Admin runs: crablet approve ABC123
3. User is added to whitelist with assigned role

docs.title: Role-Based Permissions
docs.text: Role Permissions Admin Full access, user management, config changes ReadWrite Chat, tool execution, file access ReadOnly Chat only, no tool execution ToolExecution Chat + approved tools AgentSpawn Can spawn sub-agents ConfigModify Can modify runtime configuration

docs.title: Safety Levels
docs.text: See Configuration → Safety Levels for detailed configuration.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Production Checklist

  • Set safety.level = "Strict"
  • Define explicit allowed_commands
  • Block dangerous commands: rm -rf, mkfs, dd
  • Restrict file paths to application directories
  • Enable audit logging
  • Rotate API keys regularly

docs.title: Cognitive System
docs.text:
Crablet implements a novel three-layer cognitive architecture inspired by cognitive science, enabling the agent to think at different levels of depth and self-reflect on its own reasoning.

  • System 1 — Fast Thinking

    Rapid, intuitive responses for simple queries and routine tasks

    Three-Layer Architecture

  • System 2 — Slow Reasoning

    Deliberate, step-by-step reasoning for complex problems

    Three-Layer Architecture

  • System 3 — Meta-Cognition

    Self-monitoring, reflection, and continuous improvement

    Meta-Cognition

  • :chart_line: Thought Visualization

    Real-time rendering of cognitive processes in the Web UI

    Visualization

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
graph TB
    Input[User Input] --> Router[Cognitive Router]
    Router --> S1[System 1<br/>Fast Intuition]
    Router --> S2[System 2<br/>Slow Reasoning]
    S1 --> S3[System 3<br/>Meta-Cognition]
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> Monitor[Monitor]
    S3 --> Reflector[Reflector]
    S3 --> Learner[Learner]
    S3 --> Optimizer[Optimizer]
    Monitor --> Reflector
    Reflector --> Learner
    Learner --> Optimizer
    Optimizer --> Router

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Input arrives → Cognitive Router evaluates complexity
  2. Simple queries → Route to System 1 for fast response
  3. Complex problems → Route to System 2 for deliberate reasoning
  4. Low confidence or failure → Trigger System 3 meta-reflection
  5. Meta-cognition cycle → Monitor → Reflect → Learn → Optimize
  6. Strategy updates → Feed back to Router for future decisions

docs.title: Meta-Cognition
docs.text: Crablet's System 3 implements a complete meta-cognitive loop: Monitor → Reflect → Learn → Optimize.

docs.title: The Meta-Cognitive Cycle
docs.text:
graph LR
    M[Monitor] --> R[Reflect]
    R --> L[Learn]
    L --> O[Optimize]
    O -.->|Updated Strategies| M

docs.title: Monitor
docs.text:
Collects real-time metrics during task execution:

Metric Description Confidence score Self-assessed certainty (0-1) Quality score Output quality rating Resource usage CPU, memory, token consumption Latency Response time breakdown Success/failure Task outcome tracking

docs.title: Reflector
docs.text:
Diagnoses problems when things go wrong:

  • Problem Classification — 5 types: timeout, hallucination, tool failure, context loss, ambiguity
  • Root Cause Analysis — Uses LLM to analyze failure chains
  • Severity Assessment — Low / Medium / High / Critical
  • Improvement Suggestions — 6 action types: retry, rephrase, escalate, simplify, decompose, switch_model

docs.title: Learner
docs.text:
Extracts patterns from experience:

  • Task Patterns — Recognizes recurring task structures
  • Error Patterns — Learns common failure modes
  • Success Strategies — Records what works well
  • Knowledge Base — Accumulated wisdom grows over time

docs.title: Optimizer
docs.text:
Applies learned improvements:

  • Strategy Selection — Picks the best approach for similar future tasks
  • Performance Tracking — Monitors strategy effectiveness
  • Adaptive Tuning — Adjusts parameters based on outcomes

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
use crablet::cognitive::{MetaCognitiveController, ExecutionRequest};

// Create controller
let controller = MetaCognitiveController::new(llm).await?;

// Execute with meta-cognitive monitoring
let result = controller.execute_with_meta(request, |req| {
    // Your task execution logic
}).await;

// Retrieve statistics
let stats = controller.get_statistics().await;
println!("Total tasks: {}", stats.total_tasks);
println!("Patterns learned: {}", stats.patterns_extracted);

docs.title: Performance Overhead
docs.text: Operation Overhead Execution monitoring < 1ms Reflection analysis < 100ms (with LLM) Pattern extraction < 50ms Optimization application < 10ms Memory footprint < 100MB

docs.title: :chart_line: Thought Visualization
docs.text: Crablet's Web UI renders the cognitive process in real-time, giving you insight into how the agent thinks.

docs.title: Cognitive Load Indicators
docs.text:
Real-time bars showing processing intensity for each system:

  • System 1 Load — Typically 5-20% (background intuition)
  • System 2 Load — Spikes to 70-98% during reasoning
  • System 3 Load — Activated during reflection (30-60%)

docs.title: Reasoning Chain
docs.text:
Step-by-step display of System 2's ReAct loop:

🔍 Step 1: Searching for relevant documentation...
🔍 Step 2: Reading API reference...
🔍 Step 3: Comparing implementations...
🔍 Step 4: Synthesizing findings...
✅ Confidence: 94%

docs.title: Meta-Cognitive Panel
docs.text:
Shows System 3's self-reflection when activated:

  • Problem diagnosis
  • Root cause analysis
  • Improvement suggestions
  • Strategy adjustments

docs.title: Frontend Components
docs.text:
The visualization is built with React + TailwindCSS:

Component Location EnhancedThinkingVisualization frontend/src/components/chat/ ActionableSmartSuggestions frontend/src/components/cognitive/ Cognitive dashboard widgets frontend/src/components/dashboard/

docs.title: Controlling the Display
docs.text:
The thought panel defaults to collapsed for clean UX:

  • Collapsed: Shows current cognitive layer label only
  • Expanded: Full reasoning chain, load meters, controls, suggestions

Smart suggestions are context-aware:

  • Code contexts → Code review, optimization suggestions
  • Data contexts → Analysis, visualization suggestions
  • Casual conversation → Hidden (no noise)

docs.title: Three-Layer Architecture
docs.text: Crablet implements a three-layer cognitive architecture inspired by Daniel Kahneman's dual-process theory, extended with a third meta-cognitive layer.

docs.title: System 1 — Fast Intuition
docs.text:
Purpose: Rapid, intuitive responses for simple queries and routine tasks.

  • Direct LLM inference without extensive reasoning
  • Optimized for latency (< 500ms typical)
  • Used for greetings, simple Q&A, and known-pattern responses
  • Low computational cost
You: Hello!

🦀 Crablet [System 1]: Hi! How can I help you today?

docs.title: System 2 — Slow Reasoning
docs.text:
Purpose: Deliberate, step-by-step reasoning for complex problems.

  • ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) loop with tool calls
  • Multi-step decomposition and planning
  • Self-consistency checking
  • Higher latency but more accurate results
You: Analyze the performance bottlenecks in this codebase

🦀 Crablet [System 2]:
  🔍 Step 1: Examining project structure...
  🔍 Step 2: Identifying hot paths...
  🔍 Step 3: Profiling memory allocations...
  🔍 Step 4: Checking async patterns...

  Analysis complete. Found 3 bottlenecks:
  1. Excessive clone() in distributed_harness.rs
  2. Blocking I/O in channel handlers
  3. Unbounded concurrency in swarm coordinator

docs.title: System 3 — Meta-Cognition
docs.text:
Purpose: Self-monitoring, reflection, and continuous improvement.

  • Monitors execution quality and confidence scores
  • Diagnoses problems when confidence drops
  • Learns patterns from successes and failures
  • Optimizes future strategy selection

See Meta-Cognition for detailed documentation.

docs.title: Cognitive Router
docs.text:
The router decides which system to engage based on:

Signal Routes To Simple greeting/small talk System 1 Known-pattern query System 1 Multi-step task System 2 Low-confidence result System 3 Explicit reasoning request System 2 Error or failure System 3

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
// src/cognitive/routing/mod.rs
pub enum CognitiveLayer {
    System1,  // Fast intuition
    System2,  // Slow reasoning
    System3,  // Meta-cognition
}

pub struct CognitiveRouter {
    complexity_threshold: f64,
    confidence_threshold: f64,
    pattern_matcher: PatternMatcher,
}

docs.title: Running Scripts

crablet script run examples/scripts/summarize_paper.lua

docs.text:
-- summarize_paper.lua
local paper = io.read_file(args.input_path)
local summary = llm.chat({
    model = "gpt-4o-mini",
    prompt = "Summarize this paper concisely:\n" .. paper
})

# Remove a task

docs.title: Hooks
docs.text: Hooks allow you to attach custom logic to events in the Crablet lifecycle — before/after tool calls, on message receipt, on errors, and more.

docs.title: Hook Points
docs.text: Hook Point Trigger Use Case pre_tool_call Before a tool executes Validate, transform, or block post_tool_call After a tool executes Log, cache, post-process on_message Incoming user message Filter, augment, route on_response Outgoing agent response Format, censor, store on_error Error occurs Alert, retry, fallback on_session_start New session begins Initialize context on_session_end Session closes Cleanup, persist

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
[hooks.pre_tool_call]
# Validate all bash commands
enabled = true
script = "hooks/validate_bash.lua"

[hooks.on_error]
# Send alert on critical errors
enabled = true
script = "hooks/error_alert.lua"

docs.title: Example Hook Script
docs.text:
-- hooks/validate_bash.lua
function on_pre_tool_call(tool_name, params)
    if tool_name ~= "bash" then return nil end

    local cmd = params.command
    if cmd:match("rm%s+%-rf%s+/") then
        return {
            action = "block",
            reason = "Dangerous command blocked: rm -rf /"
        }
    end

    return { action = "allow" }
end

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Skills are installable packages that add new capabilities to Crablet. They come in four flavors:

docs.title: Skill Types
docs.text: Type Format Runtime Use Case Executable skill.yaml Python/Node/Shell Standalone programs with I/O Instructional SKILL.md None (pure prompt) Behavioral instructions for the agent MCP Tool Config entry External process Remote tool servers Native Rust Compiled in Rust High-performance built-in tools

docs.title: Installing Skills

# From a Git repository
crablet skill install https://github.com/user/weather-skill.git

# From a local directory
crablet skill install ./my-skills/calculator

# List installed skills

docs.title: 1. Create the manifest
docs.text:
# skill.yaml
name: weather
description: Get current weather for a city using OpenMeteo API
version: 1.0.0
parameters:
  type: object
  properties:
    city:
      type: string
      description: The city to get weather for
  required: [city]
entrypoint: python3 weather.py
timeout: 10
env:
  API_KEY: ${OPENMETEO_API_KEY}

docs.title: 2. Write the implementation
docs.text:
# weather.py
import sys
import json
import requests

def main():
    args = json.loads(sys.argv[1])
    city = args["city"]

    response = requests.get(
        f"https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?city={city}"
    )
    print(json.dumps(response.json()))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: 3. Test locally
docs.text:
cd my-weather-skill
python3 weather.py '{"city": "Shanghai"}'

docs.title: 4. Install and use
docs.text:
crablet skill install .
crablet chat  # Now ask: "What's the weather in Shanghai?"

docs.title: :database: Memory System
docs.text:
Crablet implements a four-layer memory architecture inspired by cognitive psychology, providing persistent context across sessions.

  • Four-Layer Memory

    Working → Episodic → Semantic → Procedural

    Four-Layer Details

  • Memory Fusion

    Intelligent retrieval combining all memory layers

    Fusion Details

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
graph TB
    Input[Sensory Input] --> WM[Working Memory<br/>Current Context]
    WM --> EM[Episodic Memory<br/>Experience Records]
    EM --> SM[Semantic Memory<br/>General Knowledge]
    SM --> PM[Procedural Memory<br/>Skills & Procedures]

    WM -.->|Consolidation| EM
    EM -.->|Abstraction| SM
    SM -.->|Practice| PM

    PM -.->|Retrieval| SM
    SM -.->|Recall| EM
    EM -.->|Association| WM

docs.title: Quick Facts
docs.text: Property Value Memory layers 4 (Working, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural) Persistence SQLite + optional Neo4j/Qdrant Consolidation Automatic (time + quantity triggered) Retrieval Fusion (multi-layer hybrid) Thread safety Arc<RwLock> throughout

docs.title: Four-Layer Memory
docs.text: Crablet's memory system mirrors human cognition with four distinct layers, each serving a different purpose.

docs.title: Layer 1: Working Memory
docs.text:
Purpose: Hold the current conversation context and active thoughts.

  • Stores recent messages, active goals, and current reasoning state
  • Limited capacity (configurable, typically 50-100 items)
  • Fastest access latency
  • Automatically prunes oldest items when capacity reached
pub struct WorkingMemory {
    items: VecDeque<MemoryItem>,
    capacity: usize,
    decay_rate: f64,
}

docs.title: Layer 2: Episodic Memory
docs.text:
Purpose: Record specific experiences and events.

  • Stores timestamped episodes with full context
  • Enables "remember when..." recall
  • Consolidated from working memory periodically
  • Indexed by time, participants, and emotional valence
pub struct EpisodicMemory {
    episodes: Vec<Episode>,
    consolidation_threshold: usize,
    emotional_weight: f64,
}

docs.title: Layer 3: Semantic Memory
docs.text:
Purpose: Store generalized knowledge and facts.

  • Abstracted from episodic memories over time
  • Facts, concepts, relationships
  • Powered by Neo4j for graph traversal
  • Supports entity extraction and relationship mapping
pub struct SemanticMemory {
    graph: Neo4jClient,
    entities: HashMap<EntityId, Entity>,
    relations: Vec<Relation>,
}

docs.title: Layer 4: Procedural Memory
docs.text:
Purpose: Learned skills and procedures.

  • Stores successful strategies and action sequences
  • Enables "muscle memory" for repeated tasks
  • Updated by meta-cognitive optimizer
  • Maps situations to effective procedures
pub struct ProceduralMemory {
    procedures: HashMap<SituationType, Procedure>,
    effectiveness_scores: HashMap<ProcedureId, f64>,
}

docs.title: Consolidation Flow
docs.text:
Information flows upward through layers:

  1. Working → Episodic: Periodic consolidation (every N turns or N minutes)
  2. Episodic → Semantic: Abstraction over repeated patterns
  3. Semantic → Procedural: Practice crystallizes knowledge into skills

Retrieval flows downward:

  1. Procedural → Semantic: Apply learned procedures
  2. Semantic → Episodic: Recall specific experiences
  3. Episodic → Working: Bring relevant memories into context

docs.title: Memory Fusion
docs.text: Memory Fusion is Crablet's intelligent retrieval system that combines signals from all four memory layers to produce contextually relevant memories.

docs.title: Fusion Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    Q[Query] --> FW[Fusion Weaver]
    FW --> WM[Working Memory]
    FW --> EM[Episodic Memory]
    FW --> SM[Semantic Memory]
    FW --> PM[Procedural Memory]
    WM --> FR[Fusion Ranker]
    EM --> FR
    SM --> FR
    PM --> FR
    FR --> R[Ranked Results]

docs.title: Fusion Layers
docs.text: The fusion system operates across three layers:

docs.title: Layer Soul (Deep Identity)
docs.text: Core personality and long-term preferences that persist across all contexts.

docs.title: Layer User (Personal Context)
docs.text: User-specific preferences, habits, and interaction patterns.

docs.title: Layer Session (Active Context)
docs.text: Current conversation state, recent exchanges, and immediate goals.

docs.title: Retrieval Strategy
docs.text:
1. Candidate Generation — Each layer proposes candidate memories
  2. Scoring — Weighted combination of relevance, recency, and importance
  3. Diversity — Ensure varied perspectives (avoid echo chambers)
  4. Ranking — Final ordering by composite score
  5. Injection — Top-K memories injected into LLM context

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
[memory.fusion]
strategy = "weighted"  # weighted | reciprocal_rank | adaptive
weights = { working = 0.4, episodic = 0.25, semantic = 0.25, procedural = 0.1 }
top_k = 10
diversity_threshold = 0.7
recency_decay = 0.95

docs.title: Performance
docs.text: Operation Latency Working memory lookup < 1ms Episodic recall < 10ms Semantic graph traversal < 50ms Full fusion retrieval < 100ms

docs.title: :message-text: Messaging
docs.text: Crablet uses a unified Channel trait to connect to multiple messaging platforms. All channels share the same core capabilities — tool usage, cognitive processing, and memory.

docs.title: Supported Platforms
docs.text: Platform Status Protocol CLI stdin/stdout Web UI HTTP + WebSocket Telegram Telegram Bot API Discord Discord Gateway Feishu (飞书) Feishu Open Platform DingTalk (钉钉) DingTalk Open Platform WeCom (企微) WeCom API HTTP Webhook HTTP POST

docs.title: Channel Trait
docs.text:
All platforms implement the same Channel trait:

#[async_trait]
pub trait Channel: Send + Sync {
    async fn send_message(&self, msg: &str) -> Result<()>;
    async fn receive_message(&self) -> Result<Option<IncomingMessage>>;
    async fn start(self: Box<Self>) -> Result<()>;
    fn name(&self) -> &str;
}

docs.title: Choosing a Channel
docs.text:
• Development → CLI or Web UI for rapid iteration
  • Personal Use → Telegram or Discord for mobile access
  • Team Collaboration → Feishu or DingTalk for enterprise
  • Integration → HTTP Webhook for programmatic access
